claim: Esoteric programming languages are useful as out-of-distribution tests because they require normal computational primitives but are far less represented than mainstream languages.
Brainfuck is imperative, cell/tape-based, Turing complete, and has compilers, interpreters, JIT interpreters, hardware implementations, and self-interpreters listed by the esolang community.
Befunge-93 uses a two-dimensional grid and a program counter that can move left, right, up, or down; this makes interpreter execution natural and compilation harder.
The Befunge-93 reference distribution includes a reference interpreter/debugger and a Befunge-to-ANSI-C compiler.
Piet programs look like abstract paintings and are usually run by parsing image color transitions into stack operations.
Chef programs look like cooking recipes; ingredients hold numeric data and can be output as Unicode characters or numbers.
Whitespace uses only space, tab, and linefeed as syntax; other characters are ignored, and it has stack, arithmetic, I/O, flow, and heap instruction groups.
Shakespeare language source looks like plays; shakespearelang provides a Python interpreter, console, and debugger, while other SPL-to-C implementations exist.
LOLCODE has an official-looking language site, a v1.2 specification, and the lci interpreter is written in C with lexer/parser/interpreter components.
INTERCAL was designed to have almost nothing in common with existing major programming languages and still includes variables, arrays, and I/O.
